title.title: How a Blockchain Works
title.subtitle: From the trust problem to a chain you can't cheat.

trust.title: The Trust Problem
trust.description: Alice wants to send Bob $100 across borders. A simple-sounding act — that quietly relies on a long chain of middlemen, each of whom you have to trust.
trust.calloutLead: You can copy a digital photo a thousand times. Why can't you copy a digital dollar? Because
trust.calloutTail: . That someone is always a middleman.
keyPoints: Every middleman takes a fee, adds delay, and can refuse the transaction
Without a referee, two people could spend the same digital dollar twice
Trust historically meant: trust an institution
What if the referee didn't have to be an institution at all?

ledger.title: The Shared Ledger
ledger.description: Imagine ten villagers, no bank. They share a single notebook of transactions — except every villager keeps their own identical copy. When anyone writes a new entry, everyone copies it down.
keyPoints: No central authority — no one is in charge of the notebook
No one can refuse to record a valid entry
Everyone can audit the full history at any time
But one villager could secretly edit her own copy. How would anyone know?

villageLedger.calloutLead: Nine villagers hold identical entries. Mallory has secretly altered hers.

whatIs.title: What is a blockchain?
whatIs.description: A blockchain is the answer to the problem we just sketched: a shared ledger that no single party controls, where every participant holds a copy, and where the history can be added to but never quietly rewritten.
whatIs.plainDefLead: A blockchain is a form of
whatIs.plainDefTail: , running on a network where no single party is in charge.
whatIs.calloutLead: On a public blockchain you act through an
whatIs.calloutTail: — not your legal name. But every transaction you make from that address is visible to everyone, forever. Privacy comes from not linking the address to you — not from hiding the address itself.
keyPoints: Distributed — every participant holds a full copy of the ledger; no master server to take down
No control organs — no admin can freeze accounts, reverse transactions, or override the rules
Pseudonymous — you act through an address, not your legal name; trackable but not directly identifying
Append-only — you can write new entries, but old ones can't be quietly edited away
Blockchain is one kind of DLT, not the only one — Bitcoin and Ethereum are blockchains, Hyperledger Fabric and R3 Corda are non-chain DLTs

transactionFlow.heading: What is a transaction?
transactionFlow.leadA: The basic unit of activity on a blockchain. Every transaction is signed by its sender, broadcast to the network, queues up in the
transactionFlow.leadB: , and waits to be picked by a miner and bundled into the next block.
transactionFlow.cardCaption: Most transactions are payments, but anything can be one — votes, asset transfers, contract calls.
transactionFlow.footerB: . Miners pick the next batch (highest-fee first, usually) to fill a
transactionFlow.footerC: — then mine it. When the block is full, it ships and a new one starts.

hashing.title: Hashing — A Fingerprint for Data
hashing.description: A hash function takes any input — a word, a paragraph, a whole book — and returns a fixed-size fingerprint. The trick: change one character of the input, and the fingerprint changes completely.
keyPoints: One-way: you can't reverse a hash to find the original input
Fixed-size: always 64 hex characters (for SHA-256), no matter how big the input
Deterministic: the same input always produces the same hash
Avalanche: change one character, the whole fingerprint changes

interactiveHash.empty: Enter text above to see its SHA-256 hash in real time

quizAvalanche.question: What happens if you change a single character in the input of a SHA-256 hash?
options: Only the last few characters of the hash change
The hash changes slightly, proportional to the edit
The entire hash output changes unpredictably (avalanche effect)
The hash stays the same if the change is small enough
quizAvalanche.explanation: Cryptographic hash functions exhibit the avalanche effect: even a 1-bit change in the input produces a completely different output. This is what makes a hash a reliable fingerprint of data.

linkedBlocks.heading: Each Block Points Back
linkedBlocks.leadA: A block stores data plus the
linkedBlocks.leadB: . That single field is what links the chain together. Look at the colors — every block's
linkedBlocks.leadC: appears again as the next block's
linkedBlocks.footerB: If anyone changed the data in Block #1, its hash would change — and Block #2's Prev Hash wouldn't match it anymore. The chain breaks the instant anything upstream moves.

chainBuilder.heading: Reconstruct the Chain
chainBuilder.leadC: fields to figure out the correct order.
chainBuilder.subLead: Click a block to select it, then click a slot to place it.
chainBuilder.placeHere: ← Place blocks here in the correct order →
chainBuilder.resultClose: Almost! One or more blocks are out of place.
chainBuilder.resultReview: Review how Prev Hash links blocks together.
chainBuilder.perfectBody: Every Prev Hash matched the previous block's Hash exactly — that cryptographic linkage is what makes blockchain tamper-evident and immutable.
chainBuilder.imperfectBody: Start with the Genesis block (Prev Hash = 0x000000), then trace each Hash → Prev Hash connection. The chain can only be assembled one way.

insideBlock.heading: Inside a Single Block
insideBlock.lead: Five fields. That's it. Each one earns its place — together they make every block tamper-evident.

prev-hash.label: Previous Hash
prev-hash.desc: The fingerprint of the block before this one. Change anything in that earlier block and this reference no longer matches — the link breaks immediately.

data.label: Data
data.desc: What this block records. For Bitcoin: a batch of transactions. For other chains: votes, asset transfers, contract instructions — anything you want preserved.

timestamp.label: Timestamp
timestamp.desc: When this block was created. Becomes part of the hash, so editing the timestamp after the fact would also break the chain.

nonce.label: Nonce
nonce.desc: A number the miner found by brute force. It's the one input that gets adjusted until the resulting hash meets the difficulty target.

hash.label: Block Hash
hash.desc: This block's own fingerprint — computed from everything above. Becomes the "Previous Hash" of the next block.

prevHashExercise.heading: Chain the Blocks
prevHashExercise.leadStrong: exact hash of the previous block
prevHashExercise.insightBody: if you change anything in Block #1, its hash changes — and Block #2's Prev Hash would no longer match, breaking the chain instantly.

quizPrevHash.question: What is the purpose of the 'previous hash' field in a block?
options: It encrypts the block's transaction data
It links the block to the one before it, forming the chain
It determines the block reward amount
quizPrevHash.explanation: Each block stores the hash of the previous block. If anyone tampers with an earlier block, its hash changes — and every block that points back to it now points at a fingerprint that no longer exists.

cascade.heading: Try to cheat the chain
cascade.lead: Edit any block's data. The chain will show you what breaks.
cascade.moralBody: The chain doesn't stop you from changing data; it makes the change visible everywhere downstream.
cascade.idleMessage: Edit any block's data below. Watch the chain react.
cascade.cleanMessage: Hash recomputed. The chain still links cleanly — nothing else moved.
cascade.nothingDownstream: Hash recomputed. Nothing downstream to break.
cascade.brokenMessageSingular: You changed Block #{{n}}. Its hash recomputed — but the {{count}} block after it still references the old value. The chain caught you.
cascade.brokenMessagePlural: You changed Block #{{n}}. Its hash recomputed — but the {{count}} blocks after it still reference the old value. The chain caught you.

network.heading: The Network
network.lead: One machine holding a chain is fragile. But what if every machine on the network held its own copy? Switch between three network shapes below and click nodes to take them offline — watch what survives.

rows.title: Centralized = single point of control
rows.desc: One operator owns the system. Kill the centre and everything goes dark.

rows.title: Decentralized = clusters of authority
rows.desc: Several hubs. Killing one breaks its cluster but leaves the rest intact.

rows.title: Distributed = every node is a peer
rows.desc: All nodes hold the data and route around failures. This is what blockchain inherits.

rows.title: Blockchain = distributed + agreed
rows.desc: A distributed network plus a way for the peers to agree on which chain is the truth.

centralized.label: Centralized
centralized.tagline: One server holds everything. Fast and simple — but if it goes down, the whole network goes down.
centralized.lesson: Try clicking the centre node. Then try clicking one satellite. Same act, very different blast radius.

decentralized.label: Decentralized
decentralized.tagline: Several hubs share authority. No single hub controls everything, but each hub still rules its cluster.
decentralized.lesson: Click a hub — its leaves are orphaned because they only reach the network through it. The other clusters keep working.

distributed.label: Distributed
distributed.tagline: Every node is a peer with the same role. No single failure can take the network down.
distributed.lesson: Try clicking several nodes. The mesh routes around the gaps — this is what blockchain inherits.

sortSystems.heading: Sort the Systems
sortSystems.lead: Each system below sits somewhere on the spectrum. Click a system, then click the category it belongs to.
sortSystems.hintBody: The line is fuzzy. DNS is technically
sortSystems.hintBodyTail: but spreads load across many servers. BitTorrent has no center at all — every peer is equal. Bitcoin is the same idea applied to a ledger.

bitcoin.label: Bitcoin
bitcoin.hint: Every node holds the full chain

bittorrent.label: BitTorrent
bittorrent.hint: Every peer is also a seeder

centralized.label: Centralized
centralized.desc: One operator owns the system. Single point of control.

decentralized.label: Decentralized
decentralized.desc: Many hubs share authority. Each rules its cluster.

distributed.label: Distributed
distributed.desc: Every peer is equal. No central authority at all.

quiz51.question: If a malicious actor controls 51% of the nodes in a public blockchain network, what can they do?
options: They can read everyone's private keys
They can rewrite history and overrule honest nodes
They can shut down the protocol permanently
quiz51.explanation: The 51% attack is the threat model for any public blockchain. A majority of nodes can agree to a tampered version of history and the network has to accept it. That's why public chains want many independent participants — to make 51% expensive.

consensus.heading: Consensus — How the Network Agrees
consensus.leadA: A network of copies needs a rule for which copy is the truth. The most famous answer is
consensus.leadB: : nodes spend electricity racing to solve a puzzle, and the winner gets to add the next block. The cost makes cheating expensive.
why: Cheating costs real energy — measurable, expensive
Anyone can participate, no permission needed
consensus.altLead: Proof of Stake replaces electricity with locked tokens — validators put up collateral instead of burning power. We compare the alternatives in

protocols.heading: Many ways for a network to agree
protocols.leadA: Proof of Work is the most famous — and it's what we're about to watch. But it's far from the only one. Each protocol below answers the same question (
protocols.leadQuestion: which copy of the chain is the truth?
protocols.leadB: ) with different trade-offs in speed, energy, and decentralization.
protocols.footerB: actually runs on the next slide. The other approaches each deserve their own deep dive — that's a Course 3 conversation.

mining.heading: Mining — five computers race for Block #{{n}}
mining.leadA: Each miner tries millions of nonces per second, looking for one that produces a hash starting with
mining.leadB: . The first to find one wins — then the next block starts, and the next, and the next.
mining.racingMsg: All five miners racing for Block #{{n}} — first valid hash wins
mining.wonMsg: 🎉 {{name}} found a valid hash — starts with
mining.stopLoopTitle: Stop the loop after the current block finishes
mining.footerA: One miner finds the hash, the others verify it instantly (checking is cheap, finding is expensive). The winner is paid in newly-issued bitcoin — currently
mining.footerB: per block. That reward is what funds the brute-force work that keeps cheating prohibitive.

metamask.lead: A wallet doesn't hold your coins. It holds the cryptographic keys that prove you control an address on the chain. The most popular one by far lives as a small browser extension — and it has a friendly fox.
metamask.ctaCaptionA: We go deep on wallets, keys and signatures in
metamask.ctaCaptionB: . For now: install it, click around, see what an address looks like.

facts.body: Your private keys — not your coins. Coins live on the chain; the wallet just proves who controls them.
Transactions, using your private key — the cryptographic equivalent of a signature that says "this is from me".
Your browser to dApps — DeFi, NFTs, on-chain games, anything that lives on Ethereum and similar chains.

takeaways.title: Section 1 — Key Takeaways
items: The trust problem: digital value historically relied on middlemen — banks, registries, payment networks
A shared ledger removes the middleman, but creates a new problem: how do you stop someone editing their own copy?
Hashing makes data tamper-evident — change one bit, the fingerprint changes completely
A chain stores each block's data plus the previous block's fingerprint, linking the history together
Any change upstream cascades downstream — every block after it goes invalid, instantly
A distributed network multiplies one machine's chain into thousands; consensus (e.g. Proof of Work) picks the truth
